segments.title: HMS Polyanthus in Q-ship guise
segments.summary: An introduction to HMS Polyanthus as an Aubrietia-class Flower sloop shown in her disguised Q-ship form.
segments.body: The video begins by placing Polyanthus within the Flower-class sloop family and showing her Q-ship appearance. That disguise matters because these vessels could present a much less threatening target than their naval purpose suggested.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies HMS Polyanthus, her Aubrietia-class sloop type, and Q-ship form.

segments.title: A simple minesweeper built to survive a blast
segments.summary: The Flower sloops combined a straightforward single-screw, triple-expansion layout with protection intended to limit mine damage.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke describes the ships as simple minesweepers with one screw and readily managed triple-expansion machinery. Their forward section used three hull layers, while magazines were kept aft, so a mine explosion near the bow was less likely to destroy the whole vessel or ignite its ammunition.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the basic minesweeper role, machinery, multiple forward hulls, and aft magazines.

segments.title: From convoy sloop to disguised submarine trap
segments.summary: Reclassified as convoy sloops, these Flower vessels could masquerade as small merchant ships and operate as Q-ships.
segments.body: The film explains that the ships were reclassified as convoy sloops and made to look like small merchant vessels before serving as Q-ships. One example, Saxifrage, survived long enough to become HMS President and a Royal Naval Volunteer Reserve drill ship, illustrating how durable the type could be.

evidence.note: The transcript covers convoy-sloop reclassification, Q-ship disguise, and Saxifrage's later HMS President service.

segments.title: Speed, coal endurance, and concealed armament
segments.summary: The ships were sturdier and faster than their appearance suggested, but their coal capacity limited range despite a useful anti-submarine armament.
segments.body: Although designed for about 17.5 knots, the class could often exceed that figure and was described as exceptionally strong. Carrying 205 tons of coal constrained endurance, but two 4-inch Mark IV guns, smaller guns, and depth charges gave a disguised vessel dangerous surprises for a submarine.

evidence.note: The transcript gives the designed speed, coal load, limited range, gun armament, and depth charges.

segments.title: Why a small merchant-ship silhouette mattered
segments.summary: At 1,250 tons and dressed as a small merchant vessel, a Flower Q-ship could look like an unthreatening target to a submarine.
segments.body: The closing point is that the type's strength and 1,250-ton displacement were concealed behind an ordinary merchant-vessel silhouette. Even an unusual paint scheme did not immediately advertise a heavily armed naval trap, which was the practical value of the Q-ship deception.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the ships' strength, 1,250-ton displacement, merchant appearance, and deceptive paint.
